TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Advanced Knowledge

Trade Execution

When people imagine trading, they often picture flashing charts, complex strategies, and endless analysis, but the actual act of entering the market, the execution, comes down to something deceptively simple: pressing a button.

One click, and you’re either long or short.

On the surface, it’s no more complicated than sending a text message.

Yet that single act is loaded with meaning.

Execution is not just a transaction, it’s the culmination of everything that happens before it.

Your analysis, your planning, your strategy, your money management..all of it crystallizes in that moment when your finger hovers over the mouse or your thumb rests on the screen.

And this is where the paradox begins: the easiest part of trading mechanically is the hardest part psychologically.

Think about it.

Before the click, the trade is pure potential.

It’s safe inside your head, where it can be perfect.

You can imagine how well it will play out, how clean the setup looks, how rewarding it will feel when it hits your target, but pressing that button drags your idea into the real world, where the market immediately judges it.

Suddenly, there’s skin in the game and now your emotions awaken.

Fear steps in first. 

What if I’m wrong? What if it drops the moment I enter? 

That hesitation can cause you to miss opportunities you were prepared for.

On the other side, greed makes its case. 

What if I size up? What if I jump in a little early before it runs away without me? 

Both emotions push you off balance, making execution either too late, too early, or too reckless.

Execution, then, is not about the button but it’s about your ability to manage yourself in the moments leading up to it.

This is why great traders emphasize discipline over prediction.

They know that the market is uncertain, but their behavior doesn’t have to be.

Every click is a mirror.

Did you follow your plan?

Did you wait for confirmation?

Did you manage your size?

Or did you let doubt or excitement take control? Execution exposes the truth: whether you are trading your system, or your impulses.

The second layer of execution is sticking to your exit plan.

Many traders can press “buy” with ease, but when it comes time to sell, they freeze.

They watch profits slip away because greed whispers “hold a little longer.”

Or they cut winners too fast because fear shouts “don’t lose what you’ve gained.”

Exiting, too, is just a button press, but it carries the same psychological weight.

So, what’s the takeaway?

Execution is the bridge between theory and reality.

You can study charts, refine strategies, and journal for hours, but unless you can execute with discipline, it’s all just potential.

The trader who masters execution, even with a simple strategy, will outperform the one who can’t pull the trigger on the best setups.

The irony of trading is that it asks us to do something incredibly simple, while controlling emotions that are endlessly complex.

Clicking a button is easy.

Clicking it at the right time, for the right reasons, with the right mindset, that’s the real skill.

The button is always there.

The question is: are you ready to press it?

In this Notion part I will provide video examples with my considerations and executions, covering different type of trades in order to help you.

1st example: DOGE HTF LONG

2nd example: CRO HTF SHORT

Trading Sessions:

As you know, the global financial market is technically open 24H a day, but it does not behave the same way throughout those 24 hours. What really changes is not the market itself, but where participants are more active.

Banks, hedge funds, smart money, governments and whatever big entity you want to add, they all operate during their local business hours, and when they come online, liquidity, volatility, and most importantly intent, shift.

That is why traders divide the day into trading sessions. The Asian session is the first major phase of the trading day and begins when markets in Japan, Australia, and nearby financial centers open. (00:00 → 09:00 UTC)

During this time, as you can imagine, the largest players are Asian banks and institutions, with Tokyo being the most influential.

Because Europe and the United States are still offline, total market participation is relatively low. (take note of this)

This causes price to move more slowly and often in a contained manner.

Instead of strong directional trends, price frequently (it’s not a rule, we’re talking in statistical terms) oscillates within a range, creating what we can call consolidation.

From what I noticed, this session often “sets the stage” for the rest of the day by establishing a high and a low.

These levels matter later because they represent areas where early liquidity sits.

When price revisits them during more active sessions, stops and pending orders tend to cluster there so, for the concept of liquidity rotation, they tend to get targeted.

This is why traders often see London or New York deliberately push the price above or below the Asian range before making a real move.

Just observe the example I prepared for you on the right.

The asian session opens and in the following hours it produces a low and a high that establish what we can define “Asian range”.

As you can see the overall session is quite “boring” and do nothing but ranging..however, it produces 2 key swings.

When the Asian session closes, European follows, and the price gets first attracted to the Asian low and later on, when liquidity has been swept, it goes for the Asian high.

Once the Asian high or low is taken during London (European) or New York, the market has targeted a short-term liquidity objective.

That sweep is important because it removes resting stop orders and pending liquidity that had accumulated during the session.

At that point, 2 things typically happen, from what I noticed:

  1. The incentive to keep pushing in the same direction decreases, because the “easy” liquidity has already been collected.
  2. Price becomes free to reprice, either back into the Asian range or toward the opposite side of it.

This is why many traders observe that the first move out of the Asian range is often not the @real move, but rather a liquidity run.

(This particular nuance will also help us later, so take note of it)

See Asian session, therefore, as as a “building structure” session.

London (European) is the most important session in terms of liquidity because of its historical role in global finance and its overlap with both Asia and New York.

When London opens (07:00 → 16:00 UTC) , a large amount of institutional money enters the market almost at once.

This sudden influx of participation usually changes the character of price action.

Where Asia was balanced and controlled, London is often decisive/more violent, as we saw in the example above. (even here not a code rule, but talking in terms of statistics)

It is common to see price break out of the range that formed during Asia, either to the upside or downside. (opposite liquidity, 1st move “fake”, second one “real”)

This breakout is often the first real directional move of the day, and many intraday traders consider it the moment when the market “reveals its hand.” (don’t know if it’s clear)

London is also where trends are most likely to form cleanly, because volume is high enough to support sustained movement without constant whipsaws.

In here we can clearly see that when the London opens (left side of the chart) volume starts to rise and we have a strong directional impulse to the upside that dictates the following hours.

For the concept explained before, Asian session do nothing but ranging a little forming the low that, during the European session, gets targeted.

As you can see, the European session is violent since we have a huge amount of institutional money flowing in, together with rising volume that justifies this technical situation.

The intent during London is often to position for the day.

As London continues, New York begins to open, creating the London–New York overlap. (13:00 → 22:00 UTC)

This period is widely considered the most important window of the entire trading day. (and the one that I personally like the most)

At this point, the two largest financial centers in the world are active simultaneously.

Liquidity peaks and volatility increases.

During this overlap, the market often makes its most meaningful moves.

If we assume that London establishes a clear trend earlier, we can think that New York may choose to continue it, accelerating price in the same direction.

Alternatively, if price moved too far or too fast during London, New York may reverse it by first pushing price slightly further to trigger stop losses and then driving price in the opposite direction.

This is why we frequently see sharp, aggressive movements shortly after the New York open. The New York session on its own has a distinct personality as well.

Early in the session, volatility is high because U.S. economic reports are released during this window and because large U.S. institutions are placing or adjusting positions.

Later in the session, as Europe closes, liquidity starts to vanish.

Price action slows, ranges form again, and the market often enters a state of consolidation similar to Asia.

This late New York period is when trends either complete their move or stall, setting up the environment for the next Asian session to begin the cycle again. Now what you have to note down is that one of the most important concepts to understand about trading sessions is that they are not independent.

They interact with one another.

Asia often builds range and liquidity → London often seeks that liquidity and establishes direction → New York often decides whether that direction is accepted or rejected.

This is why if you’re interested in trading sessions and want to become a professional one, you do not look at price action in isolation but ask questions such as:

  • “Where price was during Asia?”
  • “ What London did with that range?”
  • “How New York reacted to London’s move?”

These are all important concepts to understand in order to build a trading thesis.

New York Open:

Now, here we are at the “juiciest” part I guess, seeing how you can use a session to plan your trades.

Asserting the fact that you can use whatever session you want for your trades, I can only talk about my experience and the most “lucrative” or the most stastically favoring session for me, has been the New York Open (NYO).

Now there are a few nuances, but let’s proceed step by step.

When New York opens, the market already knows where traders are positioned.

It knows where stops are sitting, especially around the Asian range and the London high and low.

Right at the open, price often makes a fast, emotional move but that first push is very often not the real intention of the session.

It exists to grab liquidity.

Breakout traders jump in, the stops from earlier positions get taken, and the market gets the “fuel” it needs. The mistake most people make is believing in that first move.

The edge, at least for my perspective, comes from doing nothing while it happens.

You let price run, because that run is telling you where liquidity was resting.

Once those stops are taken, you start watching how price behaves at that level.

If the move was real, price will hold above or below it.

If it was fake, price will struggle.

You will likely notice hesitation, wicks, rejection, or a fast snap back into the prior range.

That failure is the most important information of the entire New York Open. Once price comes back inside the range it just broke out of, the narrative changes.

Since liquidity has been collected, now it can move in the true direction.

That second move (away from the fake breakout) is usually cleaner, more controlled, and more directional.

That’s the move you want to be part of. Your entry is simply aligning with that shift.

If price ran above a high at the open and failed, you’re now thinking short.

You wait for price to either retest that failed level or show clear continuation away from it, and you enter in the direction of the reversal.

Your stop naturally belongs beyond the extreme of the fake move, because if price goes back there and holds, the idea is wrong.

Targets are usually obvious because price tends to travel toward the opposite side of the range or another pool of liquidity. Essentially, you’re letting other traders provide the liquidity, then stepping in once the market reveals what it actually wants to do.

REMEMBER: Not every New York Open will give this setup.

Sometimes it will just provide a boring ranging price action and you’ll remain at the charts for a “nothing burger session”.

However, when it does, the move often defines the session and you can potentially profit from it.

From the theory to the practice:

Until now we discussed about the theory and the concept behind the trading sessions, so it’s time to see things in a deeper way going into the “practice”.

The general rules to start and keep in mind before a NYO are mainly 2: (statistically speaking, as always)

  • If the price of BTC pumps BEFORE the NYO, usually it ends up opening with a dump
  • If the price of BTC dumps BEFORE the NYO, usually it ends up opening with a pump

These are the 2 rules that I use to navigate this session and that will dictate the future bias.

Later on we have another 2 rules:

  • The price usually produces 2 LTF moves that are made for confusing participants, up or down depending on the open
  • The price produces 2 HTF moves where the 1st is usually the fake one and the 2nd is, instead, the real one

Now observe carefully the example on the right.

I marked the key low at $90.100 (remember that lows and highs contain liquidity/resting orders) and the price on NYO decides to sweep it (1st LTF move).

Then it starts to do the opposite pumping and taking out the the previous 1m supply freshly formed, reversing back later with the 1st HTF move (which is the fake one).

For your setup you need a base where the price can stabilize (demand if it’s a long, supply if it’s a short) but in this example I want to show you how you can have a “reference” utilizing the prior swings created and therefore exploiting the extensions to calculate the “reference area”.

If the extensions align with the demand/supply, even better as you have more confluence (always look for extra confluence coming from majors like USDT D/ USDC D)

As you can see, after the first 1/2 hour dumping with the fake move, in the second 1/2 the real moves come producing a powerful impulse to the opposite side.

And in here we can find a small but important nuance:

The 1st HTF move usually ends up in the first 1/2 hour while the 2nd HTF move starts in the second 1/2 hour.

This is what I noticed after backtesting this strategy for several months.

Ok, now that we saw the mechanisms involved into the NYO session I guess it’s time to see how you can prepare a proper plan/setup, isn’t it?

Well, here you can choose 2 strategies:

  • Trading the highs/lows → sweeping of one of them and targeting opposing liquidity (see related section)

So, your potential playbook can be:

  • Mark Asian high and Asian low
  • Wait for London session to sweep one side (not mandatory, but it contributes to form a bias)
  • Define the bias based on the opposite side of the sweep
  • Wait for the NYO session
  • Look for the formation of a FVG (and structure shift if possible)
  • Enter on the pullback
  • Target the opposing liquidity

Or:

  • Trading demand/supplies (VAH/POC/VAL) → waiting for the price to come inside your D/S area targeting the opposing level

So, your potential playbook can be:

  • Mark demand/supply zones
  • Define the bias based on confluences and trend
  • Wait for NYO session
  • Look for a push into your area during the first HTF move produced
  • Enter eyeing the end of the first half hour
  • Target the recent supply/demand formed and more based on price action

In the example on the right you can find an ideal setup based on a clean demand area (entry point) following all the concepts explained before and where the ideal target is always the recent supply formed and potentially more based on the price action.

It’s crystal clear that if you DO NOT have a demand/supply area where the price moves into during the NYO, there is also NO setup.

Don’t try to trade just because “It’s NYO, so schematic says pump, dump, pump and dump again..” otherwise you’ll likely end up in a loss.

Trade with this idea but trade ONLY when there’s a valid setup to justify your time spent on charts, otherwise skip.

Now, to complete this paragraph I added the “cherry on top of the cake” which is made by 3 video explanations (2 discussed, 1 with only music) that will help you to understand the mehcanisms and potentially reproduce them.

It’s pretty much logical that, in case you want to test out this strategy you need time, commitment and patience.

There’s no perfect recipe, so don’t expect trading on NYO to produce immediate results (as per everything in life, isn’t it?) but if you have the desire to improve and especially to experiment your skills with something I’ve backtested, this can truly help you to level up.

My recommendations if you’re starting from scratch:

  • Observe the NYO session for at least 1 week (this will help you to get familiar with the “flow”)
  • Write everything down (every consideration, every movement)
  • When you familiarized, start trying it with very few amounts (when you put money on “the table” you’ll truly discover your real emotions)
  • Journal every good and bad decision you made on the trade (in order to review your execution)
  • Repeat, repeat and repeat again until your growth (and hopefully profitability) will be visible
